answer:
**Deor** is an Old English elegiac poem preserved in the *Old English Elegies* collection.[^1] It recounts a series of sorrows from Germanic legend—Weland the smith, Beadohild, Mæðhild, Theodric, and Eormanric—before turning to the poet's own loss of patronage, each episode punctuated by the refrain **"That passed over; so may this."** (A common variant is "that passed away, so may this," reflecting slight differences in translation.)[^2]

Here is the full poem in translation:

> [p-1308a0] Weland knew well the worm’s cruel bite,[^3]  
> a resolute earl endured his hardships,  
> had for his company sorrow and longing,  
> winter-cold misery; woes he often met  
> after Niðhad had laid heavy needs on him,  
> supple sinew-bonds on a better man.  
> That passed over; so may this.  
> 
> [p-d11ebc] Beadohild's heart for her brothers' death  
> was not so sore as for her own self's sorrow,  
> once she had seen with certainty  
> that she was with child; nor could she ever  
> face with firmness what would come of that.  
> That passed over; so may this.  
> 
> [p-5821a4] We have heard tell of Mæðhilde's plight,  
> how the love of Geat grew boundless,  
> so that sorrow-love stole all their sleep.  
> That passed over; so may this.  
> 
> [p-ac23ab] Theodric held for thirty winters  
> the Mærings' stronghold; to many that was known.  
> That passed over; so may this.  
> 
> [p-160f1c] We have heard tell of Eormanric's  
> wolfish mind; he ruled far and wide  
> the Gothic kingdom. A grim king was he.  
> Many a man sat by sorrow bound,  
> woe in his heart, wishing often  
> that the kingdom itself might be overcome.  
> That passed over; so may this.  
> 
> [p-b4d6b7] The sorrowful man sits, stripped of all joys,  
> his spirit darkens; to himself it seems  
> his share of hardships shall have no end.  
> Then he may think that through this world  
> the wise Lord wanders, ever-changing,  
> to many an earl showing His honor,  
> sure happiness, but to some, a share of woe.  
> 
> [p-6b7e51] This I can say about my own self,  
> that for a time I was the Heodenings' scop,  
> dear to my lord. **Deor** was my name.  
> For many winters I had a goodly following,  
> a loyal lord, until Heorrenda now,  
> that song-crafty man, received the land-right  
> which the protector of earls had granted me before.  
> That passed over; so may this.[^4]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, ch. 13 (/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/).

[^2]: The refrain appears verbatim as **"That passed over; so may this."** in this translation; cf. [p-1308a0](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-1308a0), [p-d11ebc](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-d11ebc), etc.

[^3]: Opening on Weland (Wayland the Smith), a staple of Germanic myth. [p-1308a0](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-1308a0)

[^4]: Closing revelation of the poet's name and personal lament. [p-6b7e51](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/13-welund-him-be-wurman-wraeces-cunnade/translation/#p-6b7e51)
